questions.stem: A developer wants to programmatically send a message to an agent and receive the response in their Python application. Which SDK method initiates a conversation turn?
questions.explanation: The Python SDK's conversation API uses client.conversations.send_message() to send a user message to an active session. This method sends the message, waits for the agent to process it, and returns the agent's response. You first create a session with client.conversations.create_session(), then send messages within that session.

questions.stem: To receive agent responses token-by-token in real time, the Python SDK provides a ________ method that yields response chunks as they are generated by the LLM.
questions.explanation: The Python SDK provides streaming methods (like send_message_stream()) that yield response chunks as they are generated. This enables real-time display of agent responses in custom applications, similar to how Studio's chat preview shows responses appearing word-by-word. Streaming is implemented via Server-Sent Events (SSE) over HTTP.

questions.stem: A team wants to automate deployment of agents across environments using CI/CD. Which Python SDK capability supports this workflow?

options.text: The management API: client.projects.create_deployment() with environment and agent version manifest parameters
The conversation API: client.conversations.deploy() with an environment parameter
The SDK does not support deployment -- you must use the REST API directly

questions.explanation: The Python SDK's management API wraps all project and deployment operations. client.projects.create_deployment() accepts an environment, entry agent name, and agent version manifest to create deployments programmatically. This enables CI/CD pipelines to automate version creation, deployment, and promotion across dev/staging/production environments.

questions.stem: When initializing the Python SDK client, which authentication method does it use to connect to Agent Platform?

options.text: Username and password that are exchanged for a session cookie
An API key passed as a Bearer token in the Authorization header
OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow with client ID and secret

questions.explanation: The Python SDK authenticates using project-scoped API keys. You create API keys in Studio under Project Settings > API Keys, then pass the key when initializing the SDK client. The SDK automatically includes the key as a Bearer token in every request's Authorization header.

questions.stem: A QA engineer wants to write automated tests that send predefined scenarios to an agent and validate the responses. Which SDK pattern is most appropriate?

options.text: Use the eval framework API to create personas, scenarios, and evaluators, then run eval sets programmatically
Use the streaming API to replay recorded conversations and compare outputs
Use the management API to deploy the agent and manually test each scenario in Studio

questions.explanation: The Python SDK provides full access to the evaluation framework API. QA engineers can programmatically create personas (synthetic user profiles), scenarios (test conversation scripts), and evaluators (automated scoring judges), bundle them into eval sets, and trigger eval runs. Results include per-conversation scores, pass/fail rates, and score distributions -- enabling systematic agent quality validation in CI/CD pipelines.
